entries.notes: Gold stripe color form of Premnas biaculeatus; also reclassified as Amphiprion biaculeatus in 2021 phylogenetic analysis. Confirmed by Sea and Reef, ORA, SaltwaterAquarium.com, Reef2Reef (5 total). pvp=3, sc=5, score=8.
Wikipedia article is 'Reef triggerfish' (Humu humu), the state fish of Hawaii. Confirmed by Reef2Reef, FishBase (GBIF), FWS, Aquatics Unlimited. pvp=4, sc=4, score=8.
Wikipedia article 'Flame angelfish' covers Centropyge loricula (= Centropyge loriculus per FishBase).
Wikipedia article 'Foxface rabbitfish' covers Siganus vulpinus.
One of the most popular saltwater dragonets; difficult feeder requiring live copepods. Confirmed by BlueZoo, FishBase, FishLore, Animal Diversity Web. pvp=3, sc=4, score=7.
Well-known captive shark species; endemic to western coast of North America; requires 300+ gallon tank. Confirmed by FishBase, FWS, Florida Museum, Reef2Reef. pvp=3, sc=4, score=7.
Banggai cardinalfish; IUCN Endangered due to limited range and aquarium trade pressure. Also known as Kaudern's cardinal.
Common/spotted seahorse. IUCN Vulnerable. Also known as yellow seahorse or estuary seahorse.

Lightning morph of maroon clownfish (spine-cheeked anemonefish). Genus reclassified from Premnas to Amphiprion in 2021. 'Lightning' refers to broken/jagged white bar pattern selectively bred from Papua New Guinea wild-caught fish. taxonFitConfidence medium as it's a captive color morph variant rather than a separate species.
Iconic Hawaiian surgeonfish, most popular tang in the hobby. Wide Indo-Pacific distribution despite common name. Reef-safe, herbivore.
Black color morph of Amphiprion ocellaris (false percula clownfish). Distinct from true percula (A. percula). Wikipedia URL is for the parent species.
Juvenile/subadult color phase of Rhinomuraena quaesita; adult males are blue, females are yellow. Same species as Blue Ribbon Eel.
Atlantic Blue Tang; also known as Blue Doctorfish. Western Atlantic species widely kept in marine aquariums.
Bluespotted ribbontail ray; elasmobranch sold in the marine aquarium trade. Large blue spots on grey-brown disc. Venomous tail spine.
Adult male color phase of Rhinomuraena quaesita. Same species as Black Ribbon Eel (juvenile phase) and Yellow Ribbon Eel (female phase).
Short-nose unicornfish with distinctive blue peduncle spines. Requires very large aquarium (360+ gallons). Same species as Blue Unicorn Tang.
Wikipedia article title is 'Foureye butterflyfish'.
Most common garden eel in aquarium trade is Heteroconger hassi; Wikipedia article 'Spotted garden eel' covers this species.
Also known as Dusky-finned Bullseye and Glasseye Snapper; nocturnal; all-tropical-seas distribution. Confirmed by BlueZoo, FWS, FishBase, reefs.com. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.

Also called Yellow Assessor or Yellow Scissors-tail Basslet; GBR and Coral Sea endemic; often observed hanging upside down. Confirmed by Ultimate Reef, liverocknreef, aquaticlog, AquariumWiki. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Designer ORA morph of Premnas biaculeatus; body transitions from white to shimmering gold at maturity. Confirmed by ORA, SaltwaterAquarium.com, freakincorals. pvp=3, sc=3, score=6.
Also called Goldspotted Spinefoot or Spotted Rabbitfish; Indo-West Pacific; grows to 40 cm. Confirmed by ReefGuide, Reef Life Survey, Australian Museum, Aquatics Unlimited. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Wikipedia article is titled 'Bluecheek butterflyfish'; Red Sea and Gulf of Aden endemic. Confirmed by Reef2Reef, SaltwaterFish.com, ReefApp, MarineBio. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.

Western Atlantic species from New England to Brazil; juveniles nearly black with yellow bands (source of 'Black Angelfish' trade name). Confirmed by Florida Museum, FWS, ReefGuide, MarineBio. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Also called Blue-Green Chromis; very popular peaceful reef fish; Indo-Pacific. Confirmed by Fishkeeping World, FishLore, AquariumWiki, SaltwaterFish.com. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Also classified as Tigrigobius multifasciatus after genus reclassification; western Atlantic Caribbean species. Confirmed by BlueZoo, FWS, ORA, reeflex. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Rare deep-water angelfish from Phoenix Islands; prized rare aquarium specimen. Confirmed by BlueZoo, FishBase, Reef Builders, Monaco Nature Encyclopedia. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Also called Pearlscale Angelfish; alternate trade spelling Centropyge vroliki; Indo-Pacific. Confirmed by FishBase, Reef2Reef, centropyge.net, SaltwaterFish.com. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Western Atlantic sea bass; simultaneous hermaphrodite. Confirmed by FishBase, FWS, reefguide, Aquatics Unlimited. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Western Pacific wrasse-family fish with striking orange-blue banding. Confirmed by ReefGuide, Animal Diversity Web, National Aquarium, ReefApp. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Also called Helfrich's Dartfish; striking purple-yellow-white coloration; deep water 25–90 m; western Pacific. Confirmed by Reeflings, SaltwaterFish.com, ReefPro, Reef Builders. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.

Also known as Sea Goldie and Scalefin Anthias; one of the most common aquarium Anthias. Wikipedia article is 'Sea goldie'. Confirmed by ReefApp, Aquatics Unlimited, Sustainable Aquatics, WorldwideCoral. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.

Also known as Pacific Seahorse or Giant Seahorse; largest seahorse species; IUCN Vulnerable; Eastern Pacific endemic. Wikipedia article is 'Pacific seahorse'. Confirmed by seahorse.com, FWS, marinebio, AquariumWiki. pvp=2, sc=4, score=6.
Also spelled Equetus lanceolatus in older literature; current valid name is Eques lanceolatus. Western Atlantic drum/croaker family.
Also known as Hawaiian Dragon Moray or Dragon Moray Eel. Wikipedia article title is 'Leopard moray eel'.
Also known as Yellowtail Damselfish. Caribbean species; juveniles black with blue spots, adults change to brown/yellow.
Also known as semicircled angelfish. Large Indo-West Pacific angelfish; not reef safe.
Also known as blackstriped angelfish. Sexually dimorphic; reef safe. Sequential hermaphrodite.
LiveAquaria lists as 'Chaetodon rafflesi' (single i); correct spelling is rafflesii. Also known as Raffles' coralfish.
Also known as brown surgeonfish or spot-cheeked tang. Wide Indo-Pacific distribution.

Also called leaf scorpionfish or paperfish. Sole member of genus Taenianotus. Venomous. Listed in scorpionfish category on LiveAquaria; taxonFitConfidence medium as a scorpionfish not strictly a typical 'reef fish'. Wikipedia article at genus level 'Taenianotus'.
Also known as northern seahorse or spotted seahorse. Western Atlantic. IUCN Vulnerable.
Also known as scribbled rabbitfish. Venomous fin spines. Indo-Pacific distribution.
Expert-only care; releases lethal ostracitoxin when stressed or dies. Indo-Pacific boxfish.
Also known as yellowbar angelfish. Western Indian Ocean including Red Sea. Dramatic juvenile-to-adult color change.
Also known as brownbanded bamboo shark. Indo-West Pacific. Requires very large aquarium (360+ gal). One of more suitable shark species for captivity.
Wikipedia article titled 'Humpback grouper'; genus Cromileptes sometimes synonymised with Epinephelus.
Open-ocean species with obligate commensal relationship with sharks; rarely kept in home aquaria — noted as suitable for large display tanks only.
Red Mandarin is the red color variant of Synchiropus splendidus; shares the same species, not a distinct taxon.
Also known as Vermiculated Angelfish. Max 18 cm; blue lips and yellow face distinctive.
Also known as White Sleeper Goby, Ghost Sleeper Goby, or Six Spot Sleeper Goby.
Also known as Two-Stripe Sleeper Goby or Black-Lined Sleeper Goby.
Also known as Sohal Surgeonfish. TFH Magazine article confirmed. Red Sea endemic; notorious for aggression in the hobby.
Striated frogfish / hairy frogfish. Tiger-colored is a color morph variant of Antennarius striatus sold in the trade.
Also known as Razorfish or Coral Shrimpfish. Distinctive vertical orientation behavior.
Also known as Dot-Dash Butterflyfish or Pelewensis Butterflyfish.
Wikipedia article title is 'Lookdown'. Highly compressed silver fish; grows up to 48cm. Often displayed in public aquaria due to large adult size.

Despite being listed on LiveAquaria, this temperate Northeast Pacific species is primarily a display fish for public aquariums; rarely suitable for home saltwater tanks. Not a true eel (family Anarhichadidae). November 2025 pageview spike of 64,777 inflates monthly average significantly.
Popular beginner saltwater damselfish. Also known as Goldtail Demoiselle. Hardy, peaceful when young. Indo-Pacific.
One of the most aquarium-suitable morays. Monotypic genus. Feeds primarily on crustaceans, making it safer with fish tankmates than most morays. Hardy and adaptable.
Also known as Spotfin Lionfish or Banded Lionfish. Reclassified to genus Pteropterus in 2023 per some sources; Wikipedia retains Pterois antennata.
LiveAquaria's 'Arothron Puffer' product page maps specifically to Arothron meleagris (Guineafowl/Golden Puffer). Genus-level common name used by vendor; scientific name resolved from product listing.
Notable spike in August 2025 (12,020 views) inflated the monthly average. Very popular species in public aquaria.

Designer/captive-bred morph (cross of Black Ocellaris x Snowflake Clownfish); not a wild species. Wikipedia URL and pageviews are for the parent species Amphiprion ocellaris; actual 'Black Ice' morph has no dedicated Wikipedia article. Scored using species article.
Midnight Angelfish; uniformly bluish-black dwarf angel. One of the only all-black pygmy angelfish. Western Pacific.
Black color morph of Amphiprion polymnus (Saddleback/Yellowfin Anemonefish). Captive-bred form with complete middle bar rather than the typical saddle.
Designer morph of Amphiprion ocellaris first released 2017; jet-black body with unique swirling white markings. No wild counterpart. Wikipedia URL refers to parent species.
Also known as Brown Angelfish, Phantom Angelfish. Black/brown body with ochre face and yellow fin margins. Western Pacific.
Also known as White Freckled Surgeonfish, Maculiceps Tang. Grey body with yellow pectoral fins and white facial freckles. Distinct from Acanthurus japonicus.
Also known as Maldive Anemonefish or Rose Skunk Clownfish. Orange with single white head bar and black-edged fins.
Also known as Behn's Damselfish. Attractive yellow juvenile with black horizontal bars; adults become brown and highly aggressive.
Indian Ocean/Red Sea replacement of N. lituratus; distinguished by yellow dorsal fin. Sometimes mislabeled as Naso lituratus in the trade.
Also known as Neon Damselfish. Electric blue upper body with yellow lower/posterior. Indo-Pacific.
Caribbean large angelfish; requires 250+ gallon tank. Can hybridize with Queen Angelfish to produce Townsend Angelfish.
Also known as Blue Devil, Sapphire Devil. Very popular hardy beginner fish. Males have yellow snout and tail.
Also known as Peacock Grouper, Argus Grouper. Indo-Pacific species. Considered an invasive species in Hawaii.
Endemic to Gulf of California/Baja California. Brilliant orange-and-blue coloration; burrowing species requiring deep sand bed. Cold-water species.
Also known as Majestic Angelfish. Bold yellow and blue patterning in adults. Indo-Pacific.
Also known as Filamented Gudgeon Goby, Blue Hana Goby. Pale blue body with distinctive elongated lyretail. Western Pacific.
Also known as Bluelined Hind, Boenacki Grouper. Dark brown body with thin blue horizontal stripes. Indo-Pacific.
Caribbean Atlantic species. Bright blue color; hardy schooling damselfish popular in marine aquariums.
Springer's Damselfish; brilliant all-blue coloration. Smaller and somewhat less aggressive than other damsels. Now captive-bred.
Same species as Bluespine Unicorn Tang (Naso unicornis); alternate common name used in trade. Large tang requiring 360+ gallon aquarium.
Javanese Damselfish; stunning black and electric-blue striped juvenile becomes primarily black with age. Highly territorial as adult.
Also called Yellowface Angelfish. Striking blue facial markings on yellow face in adults. Indo-Pacific species.
Also known as Dusky Cherub, Many-spined Angelfish, Brown Pygmy Angelfish. Dark blue-black coloration with blue fin margins.
Also known as Black Damselfish, Bowtie Damselfish. Juveniles are striking blue; adults darken to near-black and become very aggressive.
Also known as Blue-Striped Angelfish. Yellow/tan body with vivid longitudinal blue stripes. Western Pacific (Japan/Taiwan/China).
Also known as Blue Jaw Triggerfish, Gilded Triggerfish. Males have distinctive blue-purple throat. Considered reef-safe among triggerfish.
Also called Blue-spangled Angelfish. Endemic to Philippines. Pale orange head, dark body with dense blue spots and vivid yellow tail.
Also known as Blue-and-orange Cleaner Pipefish. Orange body with blue lateral stripes. Acts as a cleaner fish; advanced care required.
Common bluestripe snapper; also known as blue-line snapper. Can grow large (35+ cm); more suitable for FOWLR tanks.
Also known as brownbanded bamboo shark. Requires 360+ gallon tank as adult. Near Threatened (IUCN).
Also known as yellow boxfish. Secretes toxic mucus (ostracitoxin) when stressed. Expert-only care.
Designer captive-bred variant of Amphiprion ocellaris; cross of Wyoming White and standard Ocellaris. Pageviews from the ocellaris clownfish Wikipedia article which covers the base species.
Also sold as Corythoichthys haematopterus; regional variation. C. intestinalis is most common trade name.
Also known as Mimic Lemon Peel Tang or Chocolate Tang; Fijian variant name used in aquarium trade.
Common name 'Fire Coral Eel' used in aquarium trade for Gymnothorax miliaris.
Also called Barred Flagtail. Family Kuhliidae, not a true grunt (Haemulidae).
Wikipedia article 'Blackside hawkfish' covers Paracirrhites forsteri.
Designer/ORA color morph of A. ocellaris with merged wide white banding; named by Bill Addison. Confirmed by AlgaeBarn, SaltwaterAquarium.com, SeaAndReef. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Tiny 1-inch reef goby with gold-rimmed eyes and red vertical bands; Cebu Philippines origin. Confirmed by ReefApp, stlmarinesolutions, aquariumdomain. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Reddish-bronze body with golden scale edges; also spelled Centropyge aurantius; rare and challenging to keep. Confirmed by BlueZoo, FishBase, dreamaquatic, worldwidecorals. pvp=1, sc=4, score=5.
Also called Banana Eel or Yellow Moray; solid yellow body; max 24 inches; Atlantic and Caribbean. Confirmed by Aqua Imports, A-Z Animals, aquariumreefers. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Dwarf Moray or Blackspot Moray; max 26 cm; golden-yellow body; Indo-Pacific. Confirmed by ReefGuide, fantaseaaquariums, reeflex. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Sharknose Goby; cleaner fish; also known as Gobiosoma evelynae in older literature. Confirmed by fishkeeper.co.uk, TMC, algaebarn. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Gold-Spangled Angelfish; holy grail for marine angelfish collectors; rare central Pacific endemic. Confirmed by BlueZoo, Quality Marine, The Biota Group, AlgaeBarn. pvp=1, sc=4, score=5.
Also called Spikefin Goby or Flaming Prawn Goby; tiny nano goby with dramatic spike dorsal fin. Confirmed by Quality Marine, reeffishcenter, Reef Builders. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Mozambique Fangblenny; venomous fang blenny; distinctive harp-shaped yellow caudal fin; WIO. Confirmed by BlueZoo, ORA, miniwaters.fish, Quality Marine. pvp=1, sc=4, score=5.

Sold in trade as Amblygobius hectori; current valid name Koumansetta hector per Wikipedia; yellow-striped body with eyespot on dorsal fin. Confirmed by Reef Builders, liverocknreef, freakincorals. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Camel Cowfish and Humpback Turretfish; releases ostracitoxin (deadly to tank-mates) when stressed; expert-only. Confirmed by TMC, allfishes.org, FishBase. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Long-fin Bannerfish or Wimple Fish; distinctive elongated dorsal fin; Indo-Pacific. Confirmed by FishBase, Animal Diversity Web, ReefChasers. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Highfin Coralfish; Indo-West Pacific distribution. Confirmed by Quality Marine, ReefApp, TMC, GBIF. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Cubbyu or Donkeyfish (Sciaenidae); formerly Equetus acuminatus; tall dorsal fin and dark horizontal stripes. Confirmed by reefs.com, reefguide, FWS. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Honey-head Damsel or Honey-breast Damsel; Indo-Pacific algae farmer. Confirmed by Quality Marine, iNaturalist, wildsingapore. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Honeycomb Leatherjacket and Wire-netting Filefish; Indo-Pacific and eastern Atlantic. Confirmed by FishBase, FWS, ReefApp. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Orangeface Butterflyfish; Red Sea/Gulf of Aden endemic; exclusively eats Acropora coral polyps; expert-only. Confirmed by FishBase, iNaturalist, ReefApp. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Red Cheek Anthias or Threadfin Anthias; Western Central Pacific. Confirmed by SaltwaterFish.com, Australian Museum, WoRMS. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Yellow-Eyed Puffer; pale grey-brown body with yellow lips and iris; Indo-West Pacific. Confirmed by Reef2Reef, ReefGuide, Monaco Nature Encyclopedia. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Also called Blackened or Black-finned Vagabond Butterflyfish; Indian Ocean endemic. Confirmed by FishBase, Reef Life Survey, thainationalparks. pvp=2, sc=3, score=5.
Expert-only; secretes ostracitoxin when stressed. Eastern Atlantic species (Azores, St. Helena, W. Africa). IUCN Data Deficient.
Also known as cleaner pipefish or flagtail pipefish. Expert-only care; feeds primarily on live copepods.
Eastern Pacific moray eel. Smaller species reaching ~24 inches. Also known as jewel moray.
Also known as pink-speckled shrimpgoby or pink spotted watchman goby. Forms symbiotic burrow partnerships with pistol shrimps.
Caribbean sea bass. Synchronously hermaphroditic. Hardy and disease-resistant. Easy care level.
Also known as spotted soapfish or clown grouper. Toxic skin secretion when highly stressed. Expert-only care.
Also known as true lemonpeel angelfish to distinguish from false lemonpeel (C. heraldi). Reef with caution; may nip corals.
Also known as blue-and-yellow chromis or Limbaugh's damselfish. Gulf of California endemic. November 2025 Wikipedia traffic spike noted.
Also known as lined combtooth blenny. Indo-West Pacific herbivore. Easy care.
Also known as longsnout butterflyfish. Western Atlantic. One of the hardier butterflyfish for captivity.
Western Atlantic batfish. Uses modified fins to creep along seafloor; may require live shrimp to begin feeding.
Also known as lyretail pigfish. Juveniles act as cleaner fish. Indo-Pacific. Not reef safe.
Also known as moon wrasse or lunare wrasse. Wikipedia article titled 'Moon wrasse'. Aggressive toward new tank additions; should be introduced last.
Also known as blue devilfish or blue scissortail. Southwest Pacific. Swims upside down; mouthbrooder.
Also known as magnificent rabbitfish or Andaman foxface. Eastern Indian Ocean. Venomous fin spines.
Also known as marginate dascyllus or Red Sea dascyllus. Western Indian Ocean endemic.
Also known as comet fish. Wikipedia article titled 'Comet (fish)'. Mimics moray eel for defense. Plesiopidae family; not a true betta.
Western-central Atlantic dwarf goby. Sequential hermaphrodite. Very small at ~4 cm.
Also known as Japanese swallow angelfish. Strong sexual dimorphism. Reef safe. Western Pacific.

Trade name 'Midnight Arothron Puffer' refers to the all-black color form of Arothron meleagris (guineafowl/golden puffer). Wikipedia article covers the species but not this specific trade color variant.
Common name in trade is 'Miles Lionfish'; Wikipedia article and FishBase use 'devil firefish' or 'common lionfish'. Valid species, highly covered.
Wikipedia article titled 'Redtail butterflyfish'. Also known as Red-tailed or Collare Butterflyfish.
Also known as King Angelfish.
Designer morph (captive-bred strain) of Premnas biaculeatus with unique chainlink bar patterning; Wikipedia article covers the species as a whole.
Also sold as M. japonicus; has bioluminescent organs on lower jaw.
Expert-only care level; striking red-rimmed juvenile coloration fades to muted adult pattern.
Wikipedia article titled 'Coral hawkfish'; also known as Spotted Hawkfish.
Also known as Harlequin Grouper.
Endemic to Hawaiian Islands including Johnston Atoll.
LiveAquaria lists as Paracentropyge venusta; Wikipedia and current taxonomy use Centropyge venusta. Also known as Venustus Angel or Blue-backed Angelfish.
Also listed as Pseudochromis diadema; current accepted name is Pictichromis diadema.
LiveAquaria lists under dartfish collection; taxonomically a tilefish (Malacanthidae).
Only lionfish species with unmarked spines; venomous.
Wikipedia article titled 'Diana's hogfish'. Also known as Diana's Hogfish or Spotted Hogfish.
Also known as Flagtail Grouper, Darkfin Hind, or V-tail Grouper.
Also known as Eibl's Angelfish or Orangelined Angelfish.
Also known as the Sargassum Triggerfish.
Also known as Cooper's Anthias.
Expert-only care level.
Also known as Longsnout Seahorse or Brazilian Longnose Seahorse.
Common name covers multiple Rhinopias species (R. frondosa, R. eschmeyeri); Wikipedia article is for the genus.
Also known as Black Cap Damselfish.
Also known as Valentini Toby or Black Saddle Pufferfish.
Also known as Tarry Hogfish or Hawaiian Hogfish.
Also known as Indian Ocean Sailfin Tang or Red Sea Sailfin Tang.
Also known as Jewelled Blenny or Lawnmower Blenny.
Also known as Bloodspot Squirrelfish.
Also known as Red Stripe Sand Perch.
Also sold as Tahitian or Blueline Squirrelfish.
LiveAquaria product page lists conflicting scientific names (Neopomacentrus cyanomos vs Chromis atrilobata); Neopomacentrus cyanomos used per product spec table.
Also known as Duboulay's Angelfish.
Also known as Map Puffer or Scribbled Toadfish.
Northern Indian Ocean species; frequently confused with A. clarkii in the trade.
Atlantic species; five vertical black bars on yellowish body. Very high Wikipedia traffic (35876 total views / 13 months).
Juveniles have elongated 4th dorsal spine forming a banner; adults resemble typical butterflyfish shape.
Also known as Sixband Angelfish. Western Pacific; can grow up to 15 inches.
Also known as Golden Head Sleeper Goby or Blueband Sleeper. Sand-sifting sand-bed maintenance fish.
Formerly Elacatinus multifasciatus; current accepted name is Tigrigobius multifasciatus. Western Atlantic species.
Also known as Disco Blenny or Smith's Fangblenny. Wikipedia article is at 'Disco_blenny'.
Western Atlantic species. Juveniles act as cleaner fish.
Also listed as Pseudochromis splendens in older publications; current name is Manonichthys splendens.
Indo-West Pacific species; spots aligned in vertical bands interspersed with yellow.
Also known as Blackspot Angelfish or Swallowtail Angelfish. Strong sexual dimorphism.
Designer morph of the Red Sea Two-Band Clownfish (Amphiprion bicinctus). First designer morph for this species.
Western Atlantic species found in Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico.
Also known as Picturesque Dragonet or Psychedelic Mandarin. Blue coloration is cellular pigment-based.
Also known as Harlequin Sweetlips or Clown Sweetlips. Dramatic coloration change from juvenile to adult.
Also known as Pink Spotted Watchman Goby or Singapore Shrimp Goby. BlueZoo, FishBase, and ORA all confirm.
Indian Ocean species; gold ring around eye is distinctive.
Also known as Starck's Demoiselle. Bright blue body with yellow dorsal coloration.
Large pufferfish reaching up to 120 cm; reef tank unsuitable due to size and diet.
Also known as Steinitz' Prawn Goby. Lives in symbiosis with alpheid shrimp.
Also known as Pink Anthias. Indo-Pacific; best kept in harem groups.
Also called Striped Fang Blenny or Grammistes Blenny. Venomous fang blenny; captive-bred specimens available.
Also known as Hawaiian Squirrelfish. Endemic to Hawaiian Islands and Johnston Atoll.
Also known as Ribboned Sweetlips or Yellow-Ribbon Sweetlips. Species name polytaenia means 'many banded'.
Indo-West Pacific anthias; grows to 7.5 cm. Note: also listed as Pyronotanthias parvirostris on FishBase.
Also known as Peppermint Basslet. Secretive reef fish requiring 30+ gallon aquarium with cave structures. Confirmed captive bred.
Endemic to Tahiti (French Polynesia). Not reef safe. Requires 70+ gallon aquarium.
Also known as Tail-spot Combtooth-Blenny. Herbivorous reef-safe blenny; excellent algae grazer. Easy care level.
Yellow head with large black spot on dorsal fin. Relatively peaceful for a damsel. Named for Australian Museum director Frank H. Talbot.
Also known as Tasselled or Leafy Filefish. Master of camouflage with skin tassels. Not reef safe; will eat coral. Requires 100+ gallon aquarium.
Wikipedia title is 'Teardrop butterflyfish'. Characterized by a large teardrop-shaped black blotch on upper flank.
Wikipedia article title is 'Laced moray'. Also known as Honeycomb Moray Eel. Large moray reaching up to 3m; requires 180+ gallon aquarium.
Also known as Night Surgeonfish, Chocolate Surgeonfish, or Whitetail Surgeonfish. Plankton eater; requires 250+ gallon aquarium.
Also known as Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish or Pennant Bannerfish. Both 'Three Banded Butterflyfish' and 'Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish' listings refer to this same species.
Wikipedia article title is 'Threespot dascyllus'. Also known as Domino Damselfish. Common and widely kept; can be aggressive when mature.
Also known as Pennant Bannerfish. Same species as 'Three Banded Butterflyfish' listing; both map to Heniochus chrysostomus.
Also known as Keyhole Angelfish or Melas Angelfish. One of the larger Centropyge species. May nip at corals and clam mantles.
Also known as Ward's Tiger Goby or Tiger Sleeper Goby. Sand-sifting goby; white body with brown bands and eyespot on first dorsal fin.
One of the largest triggerfish, growing to 75cm. Extremely aggressive; not reef safe. Best kept alone in large species-only aquarium.
Also known as Mango Angelfish or Shepard's Pygmy Angelfish. From Mariana Islands. Generally reef safe when well fed.
Wikipedia article title is 'Red Sea clownfish'. Also known as Red Sea Clownfish; native to Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.
Also known as Scribbled Rabbitfish or Barred Spinefoot. Has venomous spines; handle with care.
Named for the pair of spots at the base of dorsal and anal fins. Good algae grazer; 75+ gallon aquarium required.

Wikipedia title 'Twin-spot goby'. Also known as Crab-eyed Goby or Signal Goby. Mimics lionfish juveniles. Challenging feeder; requires interstitial invertebrate-rich sand bed. Only member of genus Signigobius.
Also known as Reticulate Dascyllus or Reticulated Damselfish. Two large black stripes on pale/gold body. Hardy starter fish but aggressive.
Wikipedia article title is 'Splitfin flashlightfish'. Bioluminescent nocturnal species; expert-level care. Also known as Splitfin Flashlight Fish.
Also known as Two-lined Monocle Bream or Bridled Monocle Bream. Juveniles mimic venomous fangblenny Meiacanthus grammistes.
Also known as Orangelined or Orangetailed Triggerfish. Considered one of the most aggressive triggerfish. Emerald-green body with orange stripes. Not reef safe.
Also known as Crisscross Butterflyfish. Sources vary on care difficulty; some report hardiness while others rate it very difficult.
Also known as Bignose Unicornfish or Vlaming's Tang. Reaches 60cm; requires 360+ gallon aquarium. Can live up to 40 years.
Also known as Blackedged Angelfish. Strong sexual dichromatism. Reef safe; 125+ gallon aquarium recommended.
Also known as Guinean Angelfish. Rare in the trade; from West African reefs. Requires 150+ gallon aquarium. Not reef safe.

Wikipedia title is 'Southern blue devil'. Sold as Western Blue Devil in trade but is P. meleagris (Southern Blue Devil); the true Western Blue Devil is P. sinclairi. Endemic to southern Australia. Requires cooler water (64-72°F).
Also known as Paleface Butterflyfish. Native to Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. Uncommon in trade.
Distinctive white tail contrasting with dark body. Excellent algae grazer; 70+ gallon aquarium required.
Also known as Greyface Moray. Wikipedia article title is 'Greyface moray eel'. Medium-sized Indo-Pacific moray. Older synonym: Siderea thyrsoidea.
Also known as Gold-rimmed Surgeonfish or Whitecheek Surgeonfish. Semi-aggressive toward other tangs. Reef-compatible, moderately hardy.

Designer captive-bred morph of Amphiprion ocellaris (Ocellaris Clownfish). All-white body with orange face and fins. Not found in the wild; selectively bred by C-Quest. Wikipedia pageviews are for the parent species A. ocellaris.
Also known as Indian Yellowtail Angelfish or Smoke Angelfish. Indian Ocean distribution. One of the hardier large angelfish for aquariums.
Also known as Yasha Hase Shrimp Goby. Forms symbiotic partnership with pistol shrimp. Distinctive red-and-white striped body with high dorsal fin. Easy care, reef-safe.
Also known as Herald's Angelfish or Yellow Pygmy Angelfish. Solid yellow body with no pattern. Pacific Ocean. Reef-compatible with caution (may nip corals).
Also known as Goldsaddle Goatfish or Yellow Goatfish. Common name 'Yellow Back Goatfish' is used by LiveAquaria. Indo-Pacific species. Needs 180+ gallon aquarium, active sand-sifter.
Also known as Sharpnose Wrasse. Small, secretive, peaceful wrasse. Reef-safe. Yellow bands on reddish-brown body. Prone to jumping.
Wikipedia article title is 'Orange-spotted spinefoot'. Also known as Orangespot Rabbitfish. Western Pacific. Venomous dorsal spines; handle with care. Herbivore, needs 180+ gal.
Also known as Golden Damselfish. Yellow body with blue facial spots. Peaceful when young, increasingly aggressive with age. Hardy beginner fish.
Also known as Dusky Dottyback. Color varies from canary yellow to dusky purple depending on diet. Semi-aggressive and territorial. SW Pacific and Eastern Indian Ocean.

Atlantic yellow goatfish. Note: 'Yellow Goatfish' is also used for Parupeneus cyclostomus (Indo-Pacific); the LiveAquaria miscellaneous-fish listing most likely refers to this Atlantic species. Active sand-sifter, needs 180+ gal tank.
Also known as Forceps Butterflyfish. Widespread Indo-Pacific and Eastern Pacific. Long snout for picking invertebrates from crevices. One of best butterfly species for aquariums.

Also referenced as Dunckerocampus pessuliferus (synonym). Yellow body with red-maroon vertical bands; oval flag-like tail. Difficult to keep; requires live copepods in mature reef. Wikipedia article uses older name.
White pyramid-shaped body patch with yellow fins. Plankton feeder, often kept in schools. Reef-safe. Central Indo-Pacific. Wikipedia title is 'Pyramid butterflyfish'.
Also known as Yellowmouth Moray or Starry Moray. Common name 'Yellow Spotted' refers to the white spotted pattern; yellow coloration is inside the mouth. Large species (up to 180 cm). Indo-Pacific.

Also known as Urchin Clingfish. Family Gobiesocidae, not a true goby despite being listed in LiveAquaria's goby collection. Lives in association with long-spine urchins. Reddish-brown with yellow stripes.

Also known as Yellow Coris Wrasse, Golden Wrasse, or Canary Wrasse. Eats fireworms and pyramidellid snails; beneficial in reef tanks. Buries itself in sand at night. Eastern Indian Ocean to W. Pacific.
Also known as Yellow-edged Moray or Leopard Moray. Large species (up to 240 cm). Needs 300+ gallon aquarium. Brown body mottled creamy gold when mature with yellow fin edges. Indo-Pacific.

Largest Acanthurus species. Also known as Yellow-Mask Surgeonfish. Purplish-gray body with yellow fins in juveniles that fade to dull purple in adults. Needs 360+ gallon aquarium. Wide Indo-Pacific distribution.
Also known as Goldheaded Butterflyfish. Yellow head with white/grey V-patterned body. Indian Ocean. Not reef-safe; picks at corals and invertebrates. Minimum 100-gallon tank.
Caribbean species. Mouth-brooding male. Burrowing behavior; requires deep sandy substrate. Peaceful but jumps easily — needs secure lid. Popular in reef aquariums.

Two species share this common name: E. figaro (Brazilian, ORA captive-bred, yellow stripe) and E. horsti (Caribbean). WikiPedia article is for E. horsti. The ORA/aquarium trade commonly refers to E. figaro. Cleaner goby behavior. Wikipedia URL points to the Caribbean species article.
Also known as Orange-Lined Cardinalfish or Goldenstriped Cardinalfish. Formerly Apogon cyanosoma. Bluish-silver body with orange-yellow stripes. Peaceful schooling fish. Indo-West Pacific.
Also known as Black Combtooth Blenny or Tribal Blenny. Dark body with bright yellow-orange tail. Herbivore. Peaceful, reef-safe. Jumper — needs secured lid.
Also known as Chinese Zebra Goby. Grey-green body with dark bars and yellow-green borders. Active swimmer, reef-safe, peaceful. Jumper — needs covered tank. Pairs or groups recommended.
Also known as Sand Wrasse. Olivaceous body with distinctive black zigzag stripe from eye to tail. Needs sandy substrate for burrowing. Protogynous hermaphrodite. Indo-Pacific.
Also known as Brown-and-White Butterflyfish or Black Pyramid Butterflyfish. Indian Ocean distribution. Black body with white trapezoid band. Schools on outer reef slopes. Plankton feeder, reef-safe.
Also known as Bristletail Filefish or Matted Leatherjacket. Popular for Aiptasia anemone control in reef tanks.
Also known as Electric Blue Damsel, Neon Damsel, Andaman Damsel. Eastern Indian Ocean.
Obligate coral polyp feeder; very difficult to maintain in aquariums. Not recommended for captivity.
Also known as Arabian Angelfish. Red Sea endemic. Not reef-safe.
Also known as Coral Pigfish, Turncoat Hogfish, or Panda Hogfish.
Also known as Azure Demoiselle or Half-Blue Demoiselle. Hardy beginner species.
Western Atlantic species. Specialized coral polyp diet makes it difficult to keep long-term in aquariums.
Also known as Ringed Pipefish. Synonym Doryrhamphus dactyliophorus still in use by some sources.
Also known as Fiji Clownfish. Recognized as distinct species in 2008.
IUCN Vulnerable. Captive-bred specimens preferred. Found in Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia.
Also known as Yellowback Puller. Australian endemic (east coast).
Also called Ornate Angelfish. One of the more reef-safe angelfish species.
Also known as Oriole Angelfish or Pacific Rock Beauty.
Also known as Royal Dottyback or False Gramma. Wikipedia article title uses paccagnellorum (valid synonym). Notable February 2026 pageview spike (894 views).
Fiji endemic. IUCN Near Threatened. Also listed as Lo uspi or Siganus (Lo) uspi.
Also known as Dash-and-Dot Goatfish. Not reef-safe; eats invertebrates.

Myripristis jacobus is the Western Atlantic/Caribbean Blackbar Soldierfish. Some Indo-Pacific 'Big Eye Black Bar' listings may refer to different Myripristis species; Tank Facts confirms M. jacobus for this listing.
Also known as Twospot or Twinspot Anthias. Notable pageview spike in May 2026 (382 views).
Also known as Commerson's Frogfish or Giant Frogfish. Euryhaline; expert-level care.
Western Atlantic species; found at depths 10-180m. Purple body with black diagonal cap.
Also known as Namiye's Coralblenny or Tribal Blenny.
Also known as Black Tang or Longnose Surgeonfish. Rare and sought-after in aquarium trade. IUCN Data Deficient.
Captive-bred hybrid of Springeri x Neon Dottyback (Sea & Reef Aquaculture). Can shift color from jet black to rust. Wikipedia URL is for parent species Pseudochromis springeri.
Also known as Dusky Blenny, Brown Coral Blenny. Obligate microalgae eater; useful for algae control. Two subspecies (fuscus and holomelas).
Also known as Black Durgon. All-black body with thin white stripe at fin bases. One of the more peaceful triggerfish in the hobby.
Eastern Atlantic hogfish; also recorded as invasive in Mediterranean. Striking juvenile coloration with prominent black bar.
Also known as Redbar Hawkfish, Banded Hawkfish. Red/orange body with white vertical bars. Perching behavior typical of hawkfish.
Distinct from the Bicolor Blenny (Ecsenius bicolor); has blue head and yellow body. Indo-Pacific combtooth blenny.
Aquamarine body with distinctive black caudal blotch. Social, peaceful species; best kept in groups. Burrows in sand substrate.
Also known as Blue Blanquillo, Sand Tilefish. Blue head fading posteriorly with black lateral stripe. Advanced care; listed in dartfish section at LiveAquaria.
Also known as Coral Rabbitfish, Orange Spinefoot. Bright yellow body covered with small blue spots. Venomous dorsal spines.
Formally described in 2018; previously sold as undescribed species. Males display vivid blue throat with sailfin dorsal extension. Philippines endemic.
Also known as Blackfinned Shrimp Goby. Yellowish-green body with electric blue spots. Forms symbiotic relationships with pistol shrimp.
Also known as spotted, blue, or black boxfish. Strong sexual dimorphism. Expert-only care; releases ostracitoxin when stressed.
Also known as Tomini surgeonfish. Reef safe; excellent algae grazer.
Also known as blackbelly or black-patch triggerfish. Requires 125+ gallon tank.
Named after Dr. Kent E. Carpenter. Males display elaborate flashing behavior. Deep water species (27-45 m).
Also known as spotted sharpnose puffer. Eastern Pacific: Mexico to Panama and Galapagos.
Also known as chain moray. Western Atlantic species. Requires 125+ gallon tank.
Also known as fire clownfish or red and black anemonefish. Found in western and southern Pacific.
Also known as Indian dascyllus. Indian Ocean species; can become aggressive as it matures.
Also known as aiptasia-eating filefish or bristle-tail filefish. Popular for controlling Aiptasia anemones.
Also known as poison goby or citron goby. Secretes toxic skin mucus. Found among Acropora corals.
Also known as broad-barred goby. Capable of bidirectional sex change. Secretes toxic mucus.
Also known as the Okinawa goby. Popular nano reef fish; secretes toxic mucus.
Also known as lined or clown surgeonfish. Highly aggressive tang requiring 250+ gallon tank. Expert-level care.
Also known as convict surgeonfish or manini. One of the more peaceful tangs.
Also known as twospined or dusky angelfish. Popular and hardy dwarf angelfish throughout Indo-Pacific.
Also known as eclipse hogfish or split-level hogfish. Juveniles have distinct burgundy coloration with yellow spots.
Eastern Pacific endemic. IUCN Least Concern.
Also known as redtail or blue-throat triggerfish. Peaceful for a triggerfish; requires 180+ gallon tank.
Also known as rainbow goby or neon worm goby. High escape risk; requires tight-fitting lid.
Also known as yellowbanded sweetlips or lined sweetlips. Grows to ~3 ft; requires 500+ gallon tank as adult. Listed under grunts/sweetlips category.
Also known as Pacific double-saddle butterflyfish or false falcula butterflyfish.
Two species sold as Dragon Sea Moth in aquarium trade: E. draconis and E. papilio. E. draconis is the more commonly encountered species.
Designer morph of Amphiprion percula with extreme white patterning. Wikipedia views attributed to base species A. percula article. No wild equivalent exists.
Wikipedia article titled 'Fiddler ray' covers Trygonorrhina fasciata.
Designer morph (Wyoming White x Ultra Snowflake cross) of Amphiprion ocellaris created by ORA. Wikipedia views attributed to base species article.
Also called White Ribbon Eel or Ghost Ribbon Eel; white-cream body; Indo-Pacific; max 65 cm. Confirmed by AquariumWiki, saltwaterfish.com, reefs.com. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Also called Yellow Dascyllus or Three Spot Damselfish; not Dascyllus melanurus (four-stripe damsel). Confirmed by BlueZoo, thatpetplace, saltwaterfish.com. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.

Also called Golden Rhomboid Fairy Wrasse; Marshall Islands endemic; gold body with blue head and diamond-shaped tail. Confirmed by Quality Marine, reefprostore, Aquatics Unlimited. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Green color morph of Canary Blenny; venomous fang blenny; Tonga origin. Confirmed by Dr. Reef's, SaltwaterFish.com, sustainableaquatics. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.

Also called Green Dartfish and Green Gudgeon Goby; Indo-Pacific distribution; not to be confused with Ptereleotris uroditaenia (Bandtail Dartfish). Confirmed by BlueZoo, Quality Marine, CMA Aquatics. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Also called Pastel Green Wrasse; Indo-Pacific; useful for consuming nuisance flatworms and fireworms. Confirmed by Reef2Reef, Aquatics Unlimited, reefapp. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Also called Blackray Shrimp Goby or Striped Goby; forms symbiosis with pistol shrimp (Alpheus spp.). Confirmed by Reeflings, fisheee, tankfacts. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.

Also called False Tasmanian Blenny; Australian species in aquarium trade; Parablennius tentacularis is a distinct Mediterranean species. Confirmed by NY Aquatic, Australian Museum, reeflex. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Also called Flame Anthias; reclassified to Nemanthias ignitus in some sources; Maldives and Indo-Pacific. Confirmed by SaltwaterFish.com, reeflex, WoRMS. pvp=1, sc=3, score=4.
Also listed as Astrabe sp. on older LiveAquaria pages. ORA captive-bred available. Indo-West Pacific. Grows to only 2.4 cm.
Also known as yellowhead angelfish. Limited range: Christmas Islands and Cocos-Keeling Islands only.
Also known as millet butterflyfish. Endemic to Hawaiian Islands and Johnston Atoll. Not reef safe.
Expert-only care. Obligate corallivore that feeds on live stony coral polyps; difficult to maintain long-term. Wikipedia article may be at 'Exallias_brevis' or genus 'Exallias'.
Also known as threadfin cardinalfish. Transparent body with iridescent markings. Indian Ocean and western Pacific.
Also known as oblique-lined dottyback or lavender dottyback. Coral Sea origin. Western Pacific.
Also known as Indian redfin butterflyfish. Expert-only; obligate coral polyp feeder (Pocillopora specialist). Indian Ocean. Not to be confused with C. trifascialis (chevron butterflyfish).
Also known as atoll butterflyfish or yellowback butterflyfish.
FishBase uses genus Chelonodontops (reclassified from Chelonodon). Also known as milkspotted puffer or milk-spotted pufferfish.
Also known as Indian Ocean mimic surgeonfish. Juveniles mimic Centropyge eibli (Eibli angelfish), hence trade name.
Wikipedia common names include 'half black mimic surgeonfish'. Many aquarium trade specimens labeled A. chronixis are actually juvenile Acanthurus pyroferus.
Also known as Valentin's sharpnose puffer, saddled puffer, or black saddled toby. 'Mimic Saddle Puffer' is the aquarium trade name.
Designer/captive-bred color morph of Amphiprion ocellaris. No separate species; brown/mocha coloration from selective breeding.

Reclassified to Pteropterus mombasae in 2023 per some sources. Wikipedia article still under Pterois_mombasae. FishBase listed common name 'Frillfin turkeyfish'; trade name 'Mombasa Lionfish' widely used.
Also known as blackfin coralfish or Muller's coralfish. Endemic to Australia.
FishBase primary common name is 'Barred angelfish'; 'Multibar angelfish' listed as FAO alternative.
Also known as mustard tang, mustard surgeonfish, or whitespotted surgeonfish.
FishBase primary name is 'Neon goby'; aquarium trade commonly uses 'Neon Blue Goby'.
FishBase primary name is 'Orange dottyback'; 'Neon Dottyback' is the widespread aquarium trade name.
FishBase primary name: Garish hind. Neptune Grouper is the well-established aquarium trade name. Rare deep-water species.
FishBase lists 'Ochre-striped cardinalfish' (hyphenated). Also known as blue-eye cardinalfish.
FishBase common name is 'Old wife'. Australian endemic species; sometimes kept in large marine aquariums.
Designer captive-bred color morph of Amphiprion percula with heavy black markings, developed through selective breeding (C-Quest line).
Also known as Kuiter's dragonet. Wikipedia article exists under Dactylopus_kuiteri.
Also known as Klein's butterflyfish or sunburst butterflyfish. Wikipedia article is at 'Sunburst butterflyfish'.
FishBase primary name is 'Yellow clownfish'; Wikipedia and aquarium trade use 'Orange skunk clownfish'. Same species.
FishBase primary name is 'Red-spotted blenny'; also classified under Istiblennius chrysospilos. Aquarium trade uses 'Orange Spotted Blenny'.
Sea & Reef Aquaculture signature designer clownfish, created from Black Storm x orange Ocellaris over 7 generations. A. ocellaris base species.
Also known as striated surgeonfish / lined bristletooth. Trade name Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang is widely used.
Wikipedia article exists at genus level (Choridactylus) rather than species level; C. multibarbus is the type species and is featured in the article.
FishBase primary name: Orangespot surgeonfish. Wikipedia lists orange-shoulder surgeonfish as an alternative. Well-established aquarium trade name.
Wikipedia article title is 'blue damselfish'. The 'Orangetail Blue Damselfish' is the common trade name for the male form which has a yellow-orange tail.
Wikipedia article uses 'Orbicular batfish' as primary common name; 'Orbiculate batfish' is listed as an alternative.
Well-known species, Wikipedia article titled 'Painted sweetlips'. Grows large (to 1m); primarily a display-tank fish.
Also known as Hancock's Blenny. Found from El Salvador to Ecuador.
Also known as Blackfin Coral Goby or Panda Clown Goby.
Also known as Masked Pufferfish.
Designer morph (Picasso pattern) of Amphiprion percula; captive-bred selectively for irregular white striping.
Symbiotic with Tiger Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus bellulus).
Designer morph (Platinum) of Amphiprion percula; nearly all-white coloration developed by ORA through selective breeding of Picasso lines.
Also known as Strawberry Dottyback or Magenta Dottyback.
Also known as Cherubfish or Atlantic Pygmy Angelfish.
Also known as Royal Blue Pygmy Angelfish, Damsel Angelfish, or Whitetail Pygmy Angelfish.
Red Sea endemic; also known as Red Sea Raccoon Butterflyfish or Diagonal Butterflyfish.
Wikipedia article titled 'Belted cardinalfish'. Caribbean species; nocturnal mouth-brooder.
Also known as Lyretail Hawkfish or Swallowtail Hawkfish; distinguished by forked tail.
Also known as Tricolor Fairy Wrasse or Clown Fairy Wrasse.
Some older sources use Ophioblennius atlanticus but O. macclurei is the current accepted name per FishBase.
Also known as Yellow-edged Lyretail or Coronation Grouper.
Also known as Fire Clown or Red Saddle Anemonefish.
Also known as Starry Dragonet or Stellate Dragonet.
Endemic to Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.
Also known as Eritrean Butterflyfish or Redback Butterflyfish; endemic to Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.
Also known as Geometric Moray.
Difficult to sustain in captivity due to sponge diet requirement.
Also known as Haller's Stingray.
Medium taxon fit confidence. Scorpionfish family, not a true scorpion.
Frogfish family; Wikipedia article title is 'Sargassumfish'.
Also known as Secretive Wrasse or Striated Wrasse.
Also known as False Moorish Idol.
Vertically banded in alternating yellow and reddish tan; excellent algae grazer.
Designer morph of Amphiprion percula with irregular stripe pattern. 'Semi' denotes incomplete Picasso pattern expression.
Electric blue body with yellow tail; found in Sri Lanka and Andaman Sea.
White body with red stripe along lateral line; burrowing species needing deep sand bed. Excellent jumper.
Also known as Blue Whiptail or Double Whiptail Bream. Genus-level Wikipedia article only (Pentapodus).
Designer morph of Amphiprion ocellaris (ORA). Created by crossing Black Storm x Phantom. White body with black fins.
Also known as Sixline Soapfish or Skunkfish. Secretes grammistin toxin; name refers to soap-like lather.
Indo-Pacific damselfish; speckled brown and white coloration.
LiveAquaria lists scientific name as 'Fowleria sp.' — species uncertain; closest described species is F. isostigma or F. variegata. Wikipedia article at genus level only.
Also known as Striped Burrfish or Web Burrfish. Atlantic species with fixed spines.
Scientific name pre-supplied in candidate data; verified against FishBase and secondary sources.
Also known as Olive Chromis or Purple and Yellow Chromis. Western Atlantic / Caribbean species.
Also known as Whitetail Dascyllus or Humbug Damselfish. Very hardy but highly territorial and aggressive when mature. 30+ gallon aquarium.
Eastern Pacific species. Not to be confused with Heniochus chrysostomus (Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish) from Indo-Pacific.

Thunder Maroon is a captive-bred color variant of Premnas biaculeatus (Maroon Clownfish) created through selective breeding of Lightning Maroon Clownfish. Not a distinct species. Most aggressive clownfish species.
Also known as Hawaiian Butterflyfish. Deep-water species from Hawaiian Islands and Marshall Islands. Advanced aquarist care level. 120+ gallon aquarium required.
Almost entirely black body; venomous spines. Peaceful except with other rabbitfish. Not to be confused with Siganus uspi (Fiji Foxface).
Also known as Threeband Damselfish. White with three prominent black vertical bands. From Fiji.
Also known as Twinspot Blenny. Two black splotches on either side of stomach. Peaceful reef-safe blenny; 10+ gallon aquarium.
Wikipedia title 'Twospot hawkfish'. Cream body with rust-colored blotches; two black spots on gill plate and near tail. 30+ gallon aquarium.
Currently recognized as Pycnochromis vanderbilti by FishBase. Also known as Blackfin Chromis. Reef safe; best kept in schools of 5+.

Also known as Magenta Slender Anthias or Sherbet Anthias. Orange-purple-magenta coloration. Wikipedia pageview data only 10 months available (article created mid-2025). 125+ gallon aquarium; best in groups of 5+.
Forms symbiotic relationships with Alpheus pistol shrimp. Peaceful reef-safe goby; 10+ gallon aquarium with sand substrate.
Dark chocolate-brown body with white spots. Also known as Guineafowl Moray. Requires 125+ gallon aquarium with strong filtration and tight lid.
Also known as Halfmoon Triggerfish or Whitetail Triggerfish. Brownish-black body with yellow highlights and white tail tip. Indo-Pacific. Not reef-safe. 125+ gallon tank.
Also known as Twinspot Hogfish or Two Spot Hogfish. Bright yellow with faint red stripes and two black spots. Peaceful, adapts quickly. 30+ gallon tank. Indo-Pacific.
Also known as Eye-spot Blenny. Distinctive yellow eye ring, brown dorsal/pale ventral counter-shading. Endemic to Indonesia. Algae grazer. 10+ gallon tank.
Distinctive bright yellow dorsal fin, pink-white body. W. Pacific (Indonesia to Philippines, Palau). Reef-safe, peaceful. Jumper — needs tight lid. 55+ gallon tank.
Also known as Yellow-Green Goby. Light green body with yellow or white vertical stripes. Endemic to Eastern Asia. Reef-safe. Needs rockwork with caves and crevices.

Also known as Blueside Fairy Wrasse or Yellowflanked Fairy Wrasse. Highly variable coloration by region and sex — adult males develop yellow flank patch. One of hardiest fairy wrasses. 75+ gallon tank.
Also known as Fimbriated Moray or Golden Dwarf Moray. Bright yellow head contrasting dark body. Smaller species (~20-24 inches). Semi-aggressive. Indo-Pacific.
Originally described as Chromis acares. Maximum 4 cm; one of the smallest chromis species.
Also listed as Pycnochromis agilis on FishBase. Common name: Agile Chromis or Bronze Reef Chromis.
Also known as Bluering Angelfish. Not reef-safe. Requires 220+ gallon tank.
Also known as Blackspotted Puffer or Dogface Puffer. Personable species, recognizes owners.
LiveAquaria's 'Assorted Flounder' maps to Bothus lunatus (Peacock Flounder/Plate Fish). Genus-level common name; scientific name resolved from product listing.
Also known as Warty Frogfish. FishBase uses common name 'Warty frogfish' rather than 'Wartskin Angler'.
Also known as Threadfin Butterflyfish. One of the hardier butterflyfishes for aquarium keeping.
Also known as New Holland Multicolored Dottyback or Red and Green Dottyback. Great Barrier Reef region.
Also known as Footballer or Convict Fish. Actually a kyphosid (sea chub), not a butterflyfish. Eats Aiptasia anemones.
Also known as Hookjaw Moray. Sometimes listed as Gymnothorax bayeri in older sources; correct genus is Enchelycore.
Also known as Roseband Sharpnose Puffer or Bennett's Toby.
Also known as Bicolor Chromis or Whitetail Puller.
Also listed as Chromis retrofasciata in aquarium trade. Reclassified to Pycnochromis on FishBase.
Also known as Blackbarred Reefgoby or Full Moon Reef Goby.
Caribbean/Western Atlantic species. Also known as Ocellated Moray or Honeycomb Moray.
White-yellow body with multiple horizontal dark stripes. Wide Indo-West Pacific distribution. Best kept singly in aquarium.
Also known as Borbonius Anthias, Blotchy Anthias. Deep-water species (600-900 ft depth); rare in trade and challenging to keep. Pink with gold blotchy pattern and yellow fins.
Also known as Northern Blue Devil. Temperate Australian Plesiopidae species with blue-tipped elongated fins. Rare in trade; relatively peaceful for a grouper.
Also known as oyster blenny. Western Pacific coral reef species.
Known from Fiji and Tonga. Venomous fang blenny.
Deep-water species (>40 m); feeds on gorgonians and black corals. Named after Warren E. Burgess.
Rare species from Cook Islands; found at depths over 150 ft. Mild-mannered for a damsel.
Also known as threadfin anthias. Western Indian Ocean species.
Only hawkfish species in the Western Atlantic. Grows to 4 inches.
Western Atlantic sea bass. Simultaneous hermaphrodite.
Extremely rare species; known from a single specimen from Chile (1893). Requires cooler water. LiveAquaria may list Urotrygon chilensis under this name.
Also known as goldtail or earspot angelfish. Western Indian Ocean. Infrequently collected for aquarium trade.
Listed by LiveAquaria as Paracentropyge colini; current accepted taxonomy is Centropyge colini. Expert-only care.
Also known as blackheaded filefish. Indo-West Pacific. Orange to red body with dark head.
Pacific Ocean coral reef species. Herbivorous; feeds on algae.
Also known as striped eel catfish. Only marine catfish on coral reefs. Venomous spines; invasive in Mediterranean.
Also known as Marquesas butterflyfish. Central Pacific. IUCN Least Concern.
Also known as masked spinefoot. Usually travels in pairs. Venomous dorsal spines. Requires 180+ gallon tank.
Also known as redhead or Sri Lanka dottyback. Endemic to Sri Lanka.
Symbiotic with pistol shrimp. Seychelles and Maldives endemic. White body with red bars and yellow face.

FishBase and Wikipedia common name is 'barnacle blenny'. 'Mexican Barnacle Blenny' used in aquarium trade to indicate Pacific/Gulf of California origin. Note: FishBase also lists Acanthemblemaria maria (Secretary blenny) for this URL query; A. macrospilus is confirmed by LiveAquaria product page.
FishBase primary name is 'Coral hind'. 'Miniatus Grouper' is the aquarium trade name.
Also known as ovalspot butterflyfish.
FishBase primary name is 'Indian butterflyfish'.
FishBase common name: Narrowlined puffer (no hyphen).
Wikipedia common name is 'Triplespot blenny'. Also known as Big Spot Blenny.
Also known as blotched foxface or one-spot foxface. Wikipedia article confirms 'one-spot foxface' as a recognized common name.
Also known as spiny chromis or orange-lined chromis.
Also known as orange-spotted shrimpgoby or spotted prawn-goby.
FishBase name: Randall's prawn-goby. Also known as Randall's shrimp goby.
FishBase name: Orangeback fairy-wrasse. Endemic to northern Sulawesi, Indonesia.
Wikipedia article uses 'orange-striped goby' as common name. Sand-sifting species.
Wikipedia article uses 'crescent-tail hogfish' as primary name; also lists 'candy cane hogfish' and 'Pacific redstriped hogfish'.
Western Indian Ocean species (Red Sea to East Africa). Yellow body; Wikipedia article barely active.
Opaque body with red and white flecks; dramatic black dorsal fin. Forms symbiosis with pistol shrimp. 10+ gallon aquarium. Wikipedia article exists but very low traffic.

Vibrant yellow body with distinctive black stripe on dorsal fin tip. Similar to C. heraldi but with black markings. Reef-compatible with caution. 55+ gallon minimum. Very sparse Wikipedia article (stub-level).
Indian Hawkfish; named for ichthyologist Pieter Bleeker. No dedicated Wikipedia article found; genus article used as URL. Confirmed by FishBase and iNaturalist.
Also known as Yellow Lined Anthias, Luzon Anthias. Wikipedia article exists but has near-zero traffic (1 view recorded in period). Pink body with yellow horizontal markings.
Also known as candy basslet. Deep Atlantic waters; Florida Keys to South America. Very low Wikipedia traffic despite popularity in trade.
Wikipedia article exists but is essentially a stub with near-zero traffic (1 view recorded). Species described by Randall & Tanaka, 2009.
Species formally described in 2016; Wikipedia article exists but has very minimal pageviews. Named for isosceles triangle shape of caudal fin.
Not a true goby; member of family Caracanthidae (velvetfishes), related to scorpionfish. Venomous dorsal spines. Wikipedia article only at genus level (Caracanthus).

Rare deep-water anthias from Fiji/Tonga, seldom offered in trade. Males are magenta with yellow back; both sexes have yellow caudal fin. Wikipedia article is essentially a stub (near-zero pageviews). FishBase-confirmed species.

LiveAquaria product page lists scientific name as 'Antennarius sp.' — genus-level catch-all for various frogfish species. No species-specific identification possible. Secondary source confirms genus Antennarius on FishBase.
Listed as Pseudanthias cf. lunulatus on LiveAquaria, indicating uncertain species assignment. FishBase confirms Pseudanthias lunulatus (Lunate Goldie) as a valid species. No Wikipedia article found.
Also listed as Apogon kallopterus in older literature; current valid name is Pristiapogon kallopterus. Wikipedia article exists only for the genus Pristiapogon, not this species specifically.
Also known as Sleeper Bella Goby. Not to be confused with Elacatinus puncticulatus, also occasionally called Bella Goby. No Wikipedia article found for Valenciennea bella.
Also known as Tiger Jawfish or Gold-Specs Jawfish. FishBase lists primary common name as 'Gold-specs jawfish'. No Wikipedia article for this species.
Also listed as Apogon melas in older references. No Wikipedia article found for this species.
Also known as broad-stripe neon goby. Caribbean cleaner goby. No dedicated Wikipedia article found.
Also known as cave bass. Western Atlantic; deep rocky and reef habitats 30-60 m. No dedicated Wikipedia article found.
Previously classified as Pseudanthias aurulentus; reclassified to Pyronotanthias aurulentus (2022 revision). No Wikipedia article found under either name.
Undescribed species; referred to as Opistognathus sp. 6 or O. wassi in some sources. Described by Smith-Vaniz 2023. No Wikipedia article for this species.
Also referenced as Gobiodon ceramensis. Indo-Pacific coral reef goby. No dedicated Wikipedia article found.
Western Central Pacific damselfish. Reef safe. No Wikipedia article found.
Indo-Pacific reef-associated goby. Wikipedia only has a genus-level Istigobius article, not a species-level article.
Wikipedia article for Halichoeres marginatus has effectively no traffic (1 view in entire 13-month period). Treated as no meaningful Wikipedia article.
Captive-bred hybrid of Pseudochromis fridmani and P. sankeyi, originally created by ORA Farms. No standalone Wikipedia article exists for this hybrid.
No Wikipedia article found for Parapegasus natans (404 from Wikimedia API).
Wikipedia article for Fowleria isostigma has near-zero traffic (1 view in entire 13-month period). Treated as no meaningful Wikipedia article. Confirmed via FishBase and LiveAquaria.

Temperate Australian moray (SW Pacific); FishBase notes 'aquarium: commercial' but primarily temperate species not well suited to tropical marine tanks. The English Wikipedia 'Green moray' article is for the unrelated Atlantic Gymnothorax funebris. Only FishBase confirmed; popularityScore=0 (no Wikipedia article).

No English Wikipedia article found; also treated as synonym of Hippocampus spinosissimus per Lourie et al. 2016; Timor Sea to Bali; mangrove/muddy habitats. Confirmed by FishBase, Quality Marine, coralsfishandbeyond. popularityScore=0 (no Wikipedia article).
Western Pacific (Philippines, Palau) fairy wrasse. No dedicated Wikipedia article; genus Cirrhilabrus covered at species-list level only.
Also known as ear-spot wrasse or black leopard wrasse. Australia and Fiji reefs only. Sand-burrowing wrasse; requires deep sand bed.
Naturally occurring hybrid between lemonpeel angelfish (C. flavissima) and halfblack angelfish (C. vroliki). Not a recognized species; no Wikipedia article. Variable coloration per individual.
No dedicated Wikipedia article found. Confirmed on FishBase and BlueZoo Aquatics. Indo-Pacific; pairs preferred in captivity.

Listed as 'Parapercis sp.' (undetermined species in genus Parapercis / sandperch family Pinguipedidae). Not a true blenny; common name is misleading. No Wikipedia article due to unresolved species identity.
Also known as tiger queen anthias. Recently reclassified as Pyronotanthias lori on FishBase. Indo-Pacific. No dedicated Wikipedia article.

Despite common name containing 'goby', this is a scorpionfish (family Scorpaenidae, subfamily Caracanthinae). Venomous. No dedicated Wikipedia article (only genus Caracanthus covered). Listed in scorpionfish category on LiveAquaria. taxonFitConfidence medium as it's a scorpionfish not a goby.
Also known as diamond butterflyfish or French butterflyfish. Eastern Atlantic (Senegal to Gulf of Guinea). No Wikipedia article found.
No Wikipedia article found. Verified via LiveAquaria product page and multiple aquarium trade sources. Rare, recently documented species (Hoplolatilus oreni).
Wikipedia stub exists but has negligible traffic (4 total views over 3 months); treated as null for scoring. Also known as Painted Goldie.
No dedicated English Wikipedia article found.
Wikipedia article exists but has negligible traffic (1 view in March 2026); treated as null for scoring. Also known as Red Margin Fairy Wrasse.
FishBase lists as Pyronotanthias smithvanizi. No meaningful Wikipedia article (1 view recorded).
Also known as Purple Queen, Amethyst Anthias, or Sailfin Anthias. No Wikipedia article found.
Maximum size 1 inch; nano reef specialist. No Wikipedia article.
No Wikipedia article found.
Also known as Rao's Hover Goby. No Wikipedia article.
Wikipedia stub has negligible traffic (1 view in April 2026); treated as null for scoring. Also known as Silver-Streak Goldie Anthias or Tricolor Anthias.
No dedicated Wikipedia article found.
Wikipedia article for this species has only stub-level data (1 pageview recorded); treated as no Wikipedia article.
Species not formally described to species level in trade sources; listed as Pseudochromis sp. by LiveAquaria.
Wikipedia article has only 1 pageview recorded; treated as no Wikipedia article.
No dedicated Wikipedia article found; secondary confirmed via WoRMS and LiveAquaria product page.
Scientific name not confirmed by secondary sources; LiveAquaria lists it as a Trimma/reef goby species but no formal ID confirmed.
Wikipedia article has only 1 pageview recorded; treated as no Wikipedia article. Also known as Resplendent Goldie.
Also known as Big-Eye Dottyback. Wikipedia article has only 1 pageview; treated as no article.
No Wikipedia article found (404). FishBase confirms species.
Wikipedia article has only 1 pageview recorded; treated as no Wikipedia article. Newly described species from Philippines.
Also known as Whitley's Splitfin. Central Pacific species. No dedicated English Wikipedia article found.
Also known as Swalesi Basslet. Only mentioned on genus-level Wikipedia page (Liopropoma); no dedicated article. Deep reef species from Western Pacific.
Also known as Yellow Staghorn Damsel. Bright yellow body. Found in Western Pacific associated with Acropora staghorn corals. No Wikipedia article found.
Burnt orange/red and white body. Western Atlantic and Caribbean species. No Wikipedia article found. Hermaphroditic; 55+ gallon aquarium.
Rare species from Marshall and Caroline Islands. Purple transitioning to canary yellow coloration. No Wikipedia article found. Reef safe; 30+ gallon aquarium.

Red head with black body and bumpy (warty) appearance. Lives among stony coral branches. No dedicated Wikipedia article; genus article (Paragobiodon) exists. Peaceful and reef safe; 22+ gallon aquarium.
Also known as Weber's Puller. Listed in Chromis genus Wikipedia page but no dedicated species article found. Greyish-green body with forked tail. Reef safe; schools of 3+.
Red/black body with white vertical stripes; arrow-shaped. Also known as Whitebanded Sharpnose Wrasse. Peaceful; reef safe. No Wikipedia article found.

Formerly Apogon luteus. Pale yellow-orange fish with pale dusky brown stripes. W. Central Pacific (Philippines to Marshall Islands). Peaceful schooling cardinalfish. No English Wikipedia article found.

Also known as Zebra Goby or Manybanded Goby. Red body with blue vertical stripes. Temperate Eastern Pacific (California to Mexico); requires cooler water (68-75°F) than typical tropical setups. No English Wikipedia article found.
